name: Space & Astronomy
description: The solar system, space missions, and cosmic science

questions.q: What did the acronym 'NASA' originally stand for when it was formed in 1958?

source.notes: Replaced NACA (Committee for Aeronautics). Founded July 29, 1958.

questions.q: Which space mission first landed humans on the Moon in 1969?

source.notes: Neil Armstrong and Buzz Aldrin walked on the surface July 20, 1969.

questions.q: The Hubble Space Telescope is named after which American astronomer?

source.notes: Hubble discovered that many 'nebulae' were actually distant galaxies.

questions.q: How many orbital Space Shuttles did NASA fly during the 30-year Shuttle program?

source.notes: Columbia, Challenger, Discovery, Atlantis, Endeavour. Enterprise was a test vehicle that never flew.

questions.q: Which planet in our solar system has the shortest year?

source.notes: 88 Earth days. Closest planet to the Sun.

questions.q: In what year was the planet Neptune discovered?

source.notes: September 23, 1846 by Johann Galle. First planet discovered by mathematical prediction (Le Verrier).

questions.q: NASA's 'Curiosity' rover landed on which planet in August 2012?

source.notes: Landed in Gale Crater on August 6, 2012. Still operational.

questions.q: The average distance from the Earth to the Moon is approximately how many miles?

source.notes: More precisely: 238,855 miles (384,400 km).

questions.q: In what ocean did Apollo 11 splash down upon returning from the Moon?

source.notes: Splashed down July 24, 1969, ~900 miles southwest of Hawaii.

questions.q: Two stars that orbit around a common center of mass are called what?

source.notes: Estimated 50%+ of star systems in the Milky Way are binary or multiple.

questions.q: What is the name of the Japanese pressurized module on the International Space Station?

source.notes: Means 'hope' in Japanese. Largest single module on the ISS.

questions.q: Which planet is most famous for its prominent ring system?

source.notes: All four gas giants have rings. Saturn's are by far the most visible from Earth.

questions.q: The study of stars' physical properties and chemical composition is known as what?

source.notes: Applies physics and chemistry to understand celestial objects.

questions.q: Which type of star is the most common in the Milky Way galaxy?

source.notes: Estimated 70-80% of all stars. Cool, small, long-lived (trillions of years).

questions.q: Which famous BBC astronomy programme, hosted for decades by Patrick Moore, has aired since 1957?

source.notes: Longest-running TV show with the same presenter (Moore hosted until his 2012 death).

questions.q: Jodrell Bank Observatory, one of the world's leading radio astronomy facilities, is located in which English county?

source.notes: Home to the Lovell Telescope (1957), once the world's largest steerable radio telescope.

questions.q: Which U.S. state houses NASA's Johnson Space Center, the mission control for human spaceflight?

source.notes: Located in Houston. Launches happen in Florida (Kennedy Space Center), but Johnson controls the missions.

questions.q: Element sodium is the second most abundant element found on which planet's surface?

source.notes: After oxygen. Mercury has a thin sodium-rich exosphere.

questions.q: What is the innermost layer of the Sun called, where nuclear fusion takes place?

source.notes: Temperature ~15 million °C. Hydrogen fuses into helium.

questions.q: Approximately how long does a standard crewed mission take to reach the Moon?

source.notes: Apollo missions took ~3 days. Light takes 1.3 seconds.
